exercises.title: Gateway filter order — which fires first?
exercises.instructions:
```yaml
filters:
  - name: RequestRateLimiter  # limit incoming
  - name: CircuitBreaker      # check CB state
  - name: Retry               # retry on failure
```
A user exceeds their rate limit. Which filter fires? Does the CB see this call?
exercises.expected_solution:
RequestRateLimiter fires FIRST (first in the filter chain).
The user exceeds their rate limit → gateway returns 429 immediately.
CircuitBreaker: NOT invoked — request is rejected before reaching CB.
Retry: NOT invoked — request is rejected before reaching retry.
Downstream service: NOT called.

Filter execution is sequential and stops at the first rejection.
Rate limiter is first: protects expensive CB state tracking and downstream services
from excess traffic.

test_cases.expected:
RequestRateLimiter fires FIRST (first in the filter chain).
The user exceeds their rate limit → gateway returns 429 immediately.
CircuitBreaker: NOT invoked — request is rejected before reaching CB.
Retry: NOT invoked — request is rejected before reaching retry.
Downstream service: NOT called.

Filter execution is sequential and stops at the first rejection.
Rate limiter is first: protects expensive CB state tracking and downstream services
from excess traffic.

hints: Filter chain: sequential, stops at first rejection
Rate limiter first: cheapest check protects more expensive ones
429 returned by gateway directly — never reaches microservice

exercises.title: Complete the gateway route YAML
exercises.instructions: Fill in: CircuitBreaker filter with fallbackUri, Retry filter for GET only.
exercises.starter_code:
spring.cloud.gateway.routes:
  - id: order-service
    uri: lb://order-service
    predicates:
      - Path=/api/orders/**
    filters:
      - name: ___Breaker
        args:
          name: order-circuit
          ___Uri: forward:/fallback/orders
      - name: ___
        args:
          retries: 3
          ___: GET
exercises.expected_solution:
spring.cloud.gateway.routes:
  - id: order-service
    uri: lb://order-service
    predicates:
      - Path=/api/orders/**
    filters:
      - name: CircuitBreaker
        args:
          name: order-circuit
          fallbackUri: forward:/fallback/orders
      - name: Retry
        args:
          retries: 3
          methods: GET
hints: fallbackUri: forward:/fallback/orders → hits /fallback/orders in gateway app
methods: GET — NEVER retry POST/PUT/DELETE

exercises.title: Write a gateway fallback controller
exercises.instructions: Write GatewayFallbackController for Swiggy's gateway: 3 fallback endpoints for /fallback/restaurants, /fallback/orders, /fallback/payments. Each returns appropriate degraded response.
hints: Fallback in gateway app: routes /fallback/* forward to this controller
ResponseEntity: set appropriate status code (OK vs SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE)
Payment fallback: 503, not 200 — distinguish degradation from success

exercises.title: KeyResolver for gateway rate limiting
exercises.instructions: Write 2 KeyResolver @Beans: apiKeyResolver (from X-API-Key header) and ipResolver (from remote address). Show how they're referenced in application.yml.
hints: #{@beanName}: SpEL expression to reference a Spring bean in YAML
KeyResolver returns Mono<String>: reactive key extraction
Different routes can use different resolvers

exercises.title: Complete gateway route for Razorpay
exercises.instructions:
Write the complete application.yml gateway route for Razorpay's payment-service:
1. Path: /api/payments/**
2. Rate limit: 100/s per API key (redis-rate-limiter)
3. Circuit breaker: name=payment-cb, fallback=/fallback/payments
4. Retry: 2 retries, GET only, on SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE
5. Timeout filter: 5 seconds
exercises.starter_code:
# application.yml — Razorpay payment service gateway route
spring:
  cloud:
    gateway:
      routes:
exercises.expected_solution:
# application.yml — Razorpay payment service gateway route
spring:
  cloud:
    gateway:
      routes:
        - id: payment-service
          uri: lb://payment-service
          predicates:
            - Path=/api/payments/**
          filters:
            # 1. Rate limiting (first — cheapest rejection)
            - name: RequestRateLimiter
              args:
                redis-rate-limiter.replenishRate: 100
                redis-rate-limiter.burstCapacity: 150
                key-resolver: '#{@apiKeyResolver}'
            # 2. Circuit breaker
            - name: CircuitBreaker
              args:
                name: payment-cb
                fallbackUri: forward:/fallback/payments
            # 3. Retry (GET only — never retry POST payments!)
            - name: Retry
              args:
                retries: 2
                statuses: SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE
                methods: GET
            # 4. Timeout
            - name: RequestHeaderSize  # sets max request size
            # Timeout via spring.cloud.gateway.httpclient.response-timeout:
# spring.cloud.gateway.httpclient.response-timeout: 5s
hints: Filter order matters: rate limiter first (cheapest), then CB, then retry
methods: GET — payment status checks are GET; payment creation is POST (never retry!)
response-timeout: global timeout for all routes

exercises.title: Gateway integration test with MockWebServer
exercises.instructions:
Write a simple gateway test:
1. MockWebServer simulating an upstream service
2. Test: normal request passes through
3. Test: upstream returns 503 → retry fires → second attempt succeeds
4. Test: circuit opens after failures → fallback endpoint called
hints: WebTestClient: reactive HTTP test client for Spring WebFlux/Gateway
Test fallback endpoints directly: easy, no mock needed
Route testing: requires WireMock or similar to mock lb:// services

exercises.title: Multi-service gateway with different policies per route
exercises.instructions:
Write application.yml for 3 Zerodha services with different resilience policies:
1. order-service: strict (rate=200/s, CB=30% threshold, retry=2)
2. market-data: lenient rate (rate=500/s), NO retry (prices stale after retry)
3. account-service: moderate (rate=50/s, CB=50%, retry=3)
Plus shared fallback controller.
exercises.starter_code:
# application.yml — 3 routes with different policies
# GatewayFallbackController
hints: Market data: no retry — retrying a price request introduces latency without value
Different replenishRate: order (200/s) vs market (500/s) vs account (50/s)
Market fallback: 200 OK with empty prices (UI shows cached data indicator)

exercises.title: Gateway actuator endpoints for monitoring
exercises.instructions:
Configure and use gateway actuator endpoints:
1. application.yml: expose gateway actuator endpoints
2. Show: which endpoints expose route info and CB state
3. Write how to call them with curl
4. @SpringBootTest: verify actuator routes endpoint returns gateway routes
exercises.starter_code:
# application.yml actuator config
// @SpringBootTest gateway actuator test
hints: include: gateway: exposes /actuator/gateway/* endpoints
/actuator/gateway/routes: lists all route configurations
POST /actuator/gateway/refresh: hot-reload routes without restart

exercises.title: Gateway path rewriting and header manipulation
exercises.instructions:
Configure a gateway route with:
1. Path rewriting: /api/v1/payments/** → /payments/**
2. Add request header: X-Gateway-Auth=internal-token
3. Remove response header: X-Internal-Error
4. StripPrefix=1 to remove the /api prefix
exercises.expected_solution:
# application.yml:
spring:
  cloud:
    gateway:
      routes:
        - id: payment-v1
          uri: lb://payment-service
          predicates:
            - Path=/api/v1/payments/**
          filters:
            # Rewrite: /api/v1/payments/pay-001 → /payments/pay-001
            - RewritePath=/api/v1/(?<segment>.*), /${segment}
            
            # Add auth header for internal service communication:
            - AddRequestHeader=X-Gateway-Auth, internal-secret-token
            
            # Remove sensitive error header from response:
            - RemoveResponseHeader=X-Internal-Error
            
            # Strip prefix: /api/v1/payments → /payments (removes 2 segments)
            # Note: StripPrefix and RewritePath both available; use one:
            # - StripPrefix=2
            
            # Circuit breaker:
            - name: CircuitBreaker
              args:
                name: payment-v1-cb
                fallbackUri: forward:/fallback/payments

# FILTER TYPES:
# RewritePath: regex-based path transformation
# AddRequestHeader: add header to upstream request
# RemoveResponseHeader: strip header from downstream response
# StripPrefix: strip N path segments from the start
# AddResponseHeader: add header to the response sent to client
# SetStatus: override HTTP status code

public class GatewayFiltersExplanation {
    public static void main(String[] a) {
        System.out.println("Incoming:  /api/v1/payments/PAY-001");
        System.out.println("Rewritten: /payments/PAY-001");
        System.out.println("Header added: X-Gateway-Auth=internal-secret-token");
    }
}
hints: RewritePath: use regex capture groups for path segments
AddRequestHeader: added before forwarding to downstream service
StripPrefix vs RewritePath: StripPrefix is simpler, RewritePath is more flexible

exercises.title: Gateway vs service-level resilience — what goes where?
exercises.instructions:
For each scenario, state: gateway-level (add to gateway config) OR service-level (add @CircuitBreaker inside the service) and why:
A: Protect all incoming external requests with rate limiting
B: Zerodha's order service calling NSE's internal API
C: Flipkart product service calling internal recommendation engine
D: Rate limiting Razorpay's merchant-facing REST API
E: Swiggy restaurant service calling its own internal DB
exercises.expected_solution:
A: GATEWAY-LEVEL (rate limit all incoming external requests)
   Rate limiting at gateway: applies to ALL services automatically.
   No need to duplicate in each service.

B: SERVICE-LEVEL (inter-service internal call)
   Zerodha order service → NSE API: this is a call FROM a service TO an external/internal system.
   Gateway only sees external requests to your services.
   For outbound calls: use @CircuitBreaker, @Retry inside the calling service.

C: SERVICE-LEVEL (internal service calling another internal service)
   Product service → recommendation engine: internal call, not through gateway.
   @CircuitBreaker in FlipkartProductService for this specific dependency.

D: GATEWAY-LEVEL (merchant-facing REST API rate limiting)
   Merchants call Razorpay's API through the gateway.
   Rate limiting per merchant API key: RequestRateLimiter filter in gateway.
   Gateway protects ALL services with one configuration.

E: SERVICE-LEVEL (service → its own DB)
   Database calls don't go through the gateway.
   Use @CircuitBreaker for DB connection failures in the service itself.
   Or: rely on HikariCP connection pool timeouts.

test_cases.expected:
A: GATEWAY-LEVEL (rate limit all incoming external requests)
   Rate limiting at gateway: applies to ALL services automatically.
   No need to duplicate in each service.

B: SERVICE-LEVEL (inter-service internal call)
   Zerodha order service → NSE API: this is a call FROM a service TO an external/internal system.
   Gateway only sees external requests to your services.
   For outbound calls: use @CircuitBreaker, @Retry inside the calling service.

C: SERVICE-LEVEL (internal service calling another internal service)
   Product service → recommendation engine: internal call, not through gateway.
   @CircuitBreaker in FlipkartProductService for this specific dependency.

D: GATEWAY-LEVEL (merchant-facing REST API rate limiting)
   Merchants call Razorpay's API through the gateway.
   Rate limiting per merchant API key: RequestRateLimiter filter in gateway.
   Gateway protects ALL services with one configuration.

E: SERVICE-LEVEL (service → its own DB)
   Database calls don't go through the gateway.
   Use @CircuitBreaker for DB connection failures in the service itself.
   Or: rely on HikariCP connection pool timeouts.

hints: Gateway: protects incoming requests to your services
Service-level: protects outgoing calls FROM your service to dependencies
Both: gateway for ingress; @CircuitBreaker for egress

exercises.title: Full Swiggy gateway configuration
exercises.instructions:
Write complete Swiggy gateway application.yml:
3 routes: restaurant-service, order-service, payment-service
Different policies per service (rate limits, CB configs)
GatewayFallbackController for all 3
@SpringBootTest: 2 fallback endpoint tests
hints: Payment: no retry in gateway (POST is non-idempotent, GET status check is low value)
Restaurant fallback: 200 OK with empty list — page still renders
Payment fallback: 503 — must surface the error to user

exercises.title: Dynamic route registration in gateway
exercises.instructions: Write a GatewayRouteService that dynamically adds and removes routes without restart using RouteDefinitionRepository and /actuator/gateway/refresh.
hints: RefreshRoutesEvent: tells gateway to reload routes from repository
RouteDefinitionRepository: in-memory or persistent (Redis) route storage
Use case: blue-green deploy — switch route target without downtime

exercises.title: Razorpay complete gateway — synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build Razorpay's production gateway:
1. 3 routes: payment-service (strict), notification-service (lenient), fraud-service (moderate)
2. KeyResolver per API key
3. Fallback controller with appropriate status codes for each
4. Actuator exposed
5. @SpringBootTest: 3 fallback endpoint tests all pass
hints: Fraud fallback: ALLOW_WITH_REVIEW — safer than blocking when fraud service is down
Payment fallback: must be 503 with retryable flag
Three independent fallbacks: different criticality = different degradation behavior

exercises.title: Load balancing in gateway with health check
exercises.instructions:
Configure Spring Cloud Gateway with load balancing + health check integration:
1. lb://service-name URI: Spring Cloud LoadBalancer selects healthy instance
2. DiscoveryClient health check: remove unhealthy instances from rotation
3. application.yml: service registration and discovery config
4. Show: how load balancer integrates with circuit breaker at gateway
hints: lb://: instructs gateway to use Spring Cloud LoadBalancer
Eureka/Discovery: instances register; LB routes to registered healthy instances
CB + LB: circuit opens when all instances fail, not just one

exercises.title: Complete microservice resilience synthesis — all of Module 19
exercises.instructions:
Build the complete Razorpay resilience stack:
1. Gateway: rate limiting + CB filter + retry filter
2. Inside payment-service: @Bulkhead + @CircuitBreaker + @Retry + @TimeLimiter
3. GatewayFallbackController
4. CB event listener for alerts
5. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): gateway fallbacks work, service-level CB fires, all pass

Module 19 complete synthesis.
hints: Four stacked annotations: Bulkhead > CB > Retry > TimeLimiter (all need CompletableFuture)
Alert listener: @PostConstruct on M19AlertService
test 3: either CB state is OPEN or alertSvc.cbOpened > 0 (timing may vary)
